exemplar.title:
How do Russian state media and Putinâs administration portray the Moscow Concert
Hall attacks to frame Ukraine as responsible?

detailedExemplarData.title:
How do Russian state media and Putinâs administration portray the Moscow Concert
Hall attacks to frame Ukraine as responsible?

strengths: Logical headings (Introduction, Analysis, Conclusion) guide the reader
Generally clear language and coherent presentation of key sections
weaknesses: Frequent grammatical errors and awkward phrasing obscure meaning at times
Overly long paragraphs and footnote clutter hinder readability
Citation formatting is inconsistent, disrupting academic flow
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both communication strands were assessed at the basic-structure/clear-but-lapses level (band 2). The report is understandable and organized, but errors and formatting issues prevent a higher mark, resulting in a score of 2.

strengths: Explicitly identifies the political issue with clear, precise explanation and contextual detail
Provides specific reasons for project importance (mediaâs influence, war relevance, IR theory link)
Engagement activities are explained and linked to the research focus
weaknesses: Importance/suitability justification could deepen by tying theoretical insights to expected outcomes
Relevance of some engagement activities is more general than analytically rich
Would benefit from concrete examples or quotes to substantiate claims
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Sub-strand assessments placed two areas in the mid (3) band and one in the top (4) band. The issue is clearly stated and context is precise (justifying near-top performance), but project rationale and activity linkage remain at a solid mid-band level. An overall score of 3 reflects this balanced profile.

strengths: Demonstrates a basic research plan with mostly appropriate sources
Selects and describes purposeful engagement activities (ambassador interview, lecture attendance)
Some evidence that research findings inform engagement choices
weaknesses: Lacks a structured timeline or detailed methodology matrix
Engagement logistics (timing, preparatory questions) are underdeveloped
Integration of research into engagement is intermittent rather than systematic
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three process strands fall squarely in the âbasic/partialâ planning category (band 2). There is clear planning and some linkage but no fully fleshed-out structure or consistent feedback loop, warranting a holistic score of 2.

strengths: Accurate, purposeful application of key IR concepts (neoclassical realism, constructivism) to real-world examples
Effective selection of stakeholder perspectives (KumÅ¾a interview, EU parliamentarians)
Attempts at weaving perspectives into the argument, moving beyond pure description
weaknesses: Analysis remains largely descriptive and lacks deeper, multilayered exploration of strategic doctrines
Synthesis of viewpoints is partial; connections are not consistently fully integrated
Some repetition in concept application suggests room for more nuanced insight
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Each sub-strand was assessed at the mid-band (3â4) level, indicating adequate but not penetrating analysis or synthesis. A holistic mark of 3 reflects the solid use of theory and perspectives, balanced by descriptive tendencies and incomplete integration.

strengths: Offers genuine insights into media manipulation and suggestions for future improvements
Evaluates strengths and weaknesses of the investigation (e.g., objectivity of sources vs. need for firsthand voices)
Reflects on lessons learned and potential adjustments for future research
weaknesses: Personal and cultural biases remain largely unacknowledged
Critical evaluation of source credibility and methodological limitations is surface-level
Reflection on future implications could be more detailed and specific
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands fall in the adequate reflection/evaluation band (3â4), while awareness of bias is minimal (1â2). Balancing these yields a mid-band holistic score of 3, capturing genuine reflective work tempered by gaps in bias acknowledgement.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student states the political issue explicitly in the title and in the opening paragraph, clearly defining it as the Kremlinâs and state-media framing of the Crocus City Hall terrorist attack.  Relevant contextual details (date, casualties, basic sequence of events, long-running Russia-Ukraine war background) are provided, allowing the reader to understand why the issue matters.  The explanation is precise and remains focused on media framing throughout the paper, demonstrating full clarity.  Hence the work meets the top band.

The student outlines the intention to use content analysis of state outlets, comparative international coverage, and one expert interview.  A bibliography of varied sources is appended.  However, no structured timeline, methodology matrix, or explicit criteria for source selection is presented.  This fits âbasic plan; sources mostly appropriateâ rather than the fully âwell-structuredâ descriptor.

Evidence of planning exists through the deliberate choice of an ambassador interview and attendance at a specific EYE lecture, both aligned to the research focus.  Nonetheless, the write-up lacks details about scheduling, preparatory questions, or logistical arrangements, indicating a partially thought-through, but not fully robust, engagement plan.

Key conceptsâneoclassical realism, constructivism, power, peace & conflictâare introduced and applied to specific examples (framing Ukraine, soft power sanctions).  The applications are mostly accurate yet occasionally superficial or repetitive, meeting the âadequate, mostly accurateâ level at the lower end of the 3â4 band.

Research findings (e.g., BBC/CNN reports, U.S. intelligence warnings) are linked to comments from KumÅ¾a and to themes raised in the lecture, showing that engagement inputs inform the analysis.  However, this feedback loop is intermittent and not systematically demonstrated across the whole investigation, matching the âresearch sometimes informs engagementâ description.

Two engagement activities are described; for each the student explains why the activity was chosen and how it links to analysing Russian media narratives (diplomatic insight and EU perspective on power/media freedom).  The relevance is convincingly articulated, although the links are sometimes general rather than deeply analytical, so a mid-band score is warranted.

Meaning is generally clear, but frequent grammatical errors (âpalace of Russian rulersâ, fragmented sentences) and awkward phrasing sometimes obscure ideas.  Coherence therefore meets the 2-mark descriptor rather than the consistently clear top band.

The paper moves beyond narration by commenting on Kremlin motives, domestic legitimacy, and Western limitations.  Still, exploration of deeper, multilayered causes (e.g., domestic politics within Russia, information-warfare doctrine) is limited.  Thus the analysis is clear but not penetrating, meriting a 3 within the 3â4 band.

Throughout the text the student does not explicitly reflect on personal standpoint, cultural background, or potential biases in selecting Western sources.  As biases are essentially unacknowledged, the strand falls into the 1â2 band; the minimum score of 1 is appropriate.

Headings (Introduction, Analysis, Conclusion) provide basic organisation, yet abrupt transitions, footnote clutter, and misplaced bibliography numbering occasionally confuse the reader.  This corresponds to âbasic structure; some confusionâ for 2 marks.

The conclusion comments on strengths (objectivity of international media) and weaknesses (need for more firsthand voices) of the investigation, showing some evaluative thinking.  Yet the critique remains surface-level; methodological limitations and source credibility are not examined in depth.  This fits the 3â4 band but at its lower end.

The introduction offers specific reasons for importance: mediaâs power over public opinion, relevance of the Russia-Ukraine war, and the opportunity to link IR theory to real-world cases.  Suitability is justified by explaining the feasibility of collecting state-media outputs and expert opinion.  While effective, the justification is not exceptionally nuanced, so a solid but not maximum mark in the 3â4 band is appropriate.

Stakeholders referenced include Russian leaders, state media, Ukrainian officials, Western democracies, EU parliamentarians, and IS-K.  The student links some perspectives (e.g., Russian victim narrative vs. Western evidence-based reporting) but does not fully weave them into an integrated argument.  This partial synthesis aligns with a mid-band score.

The student articulates what was learned about media manipulation and suggests future improvements (include witnessesâ voices).  Insights are genuine but remain fairly general and do not project detailed future application, matching adequate reflection (3â4 band, lower end).

annotations.comment: Break this paragraph into shorter segments, each introducing one concept at a time, to enhance clarity and reader engagement.
The student clearly identifies the political issue in the title, explicitly framing the Kremlinâs media portrayal and providing precise focus and immediate context for the investigation.
Introducing a dedicated "Introduction" heading guides the reader effectively into the research context, establishing a logical entry point.
This opening paragraph is overly long and includes multiple clauses without clear separation, which hinders the clarity and readability of key theoretical frameworks and arguments.
A clear "Conclusion" section provides closure and signals reflection on findings, enhancing report structure.
The conclusion reiterates analysis points but lacks a concise synthesis of lessons learned; focus on summarizing key insights succinctly.
Consider elaborating on how this research experience will shape your future approach to media analysis and research design.
In this rationale paragraph, the student asserts importance but could deepen the project justification by linking specific theoretical insights to anticipated research outcomes.
Introducing a dedicated "Engagement activity" section clearly delineates research from engagement, aiding the structure and focus of the report.
Selecting Algirdas KumÅ¾a as an interviewee provides direct diplomatic insight and enhances the credibility of stakeholder perspectives in the analysis.
The description of the lecture would benefit from clearer linkage to your specific research questions, explaining how each speakerâs points directly inform your content analysis.
The clear "Analysis" heading marks a transition into core evaluation, improving the report's structural coherence.
The analysis here is largely descriptive and lacks deeper exploration of underlying strategic doctrines; consider integrating more nuanced IR theory to enrich the explanation.
This concluding sentence of the theoretical explanation could be condensed and linked back to your research question to maintain analytical focus.
This paragraph successfully applies constructivist concepts to real-world statements, demonstrating an appropriate use of IR theory in context.
The use of U.S. intelligence warnings is relevant, but there is insufficient evaluation of its credibility and potential bias; an assessment of source limitations is needed.
To strengthen this contrast, include specific examples or quotes from BBC and CNN reports to substantiate your claims about evidence-based reporting.
This section comprises a single, lengthy paragraph that covers multiple ideas; breaking it into focused sub-sections would improve readability and coherence.
The integration of insights from both the ambassador and lecture shows an attempt at synthesizing stakeholder perspectives within your argument.
Reflect on your own perspective when evaluating these narrativesâacknowledging potential biases could deepen your critical analysis.
Ensure consistent citation formatting (e.g., punctuation, italicization) across all entries to adhere to academic conventions.
Some sources in the bibliography are not cited in-text (e.g., Dictionary Cambridge entry); verify all references are appropriately referenced.
Does the bibliography include a balanced mix of primary and secondary sources to support your content analysis?
Complete the bibliography by alphabetizing entries or following the chosen style guide systematically for ease of reference.
Have you considered including local-language sources or eyewitness testimonies to broaden your source diversity?

rubricData.description: For the new Global Politics syllabus
rubricData.name: Global Politics IA: Engagement Activity

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
1. Issue not identified or irrelevant
2. No meaningful explanation
No discussion of why the project matters
Activities absent or unrelated to issue

1. Issue named but explanation vague / superficial
2. Limited or missing context
Importance / suitability mentioned but weak or generic
Activities listed; relevance implied or weakly linked

1. Issue explicitly stated
2. Clear, precise explanation with relevant context
Importance / suitability clearly justified with specific reasons
Activities fully explained and convincingly linked to the issue
No link between research and engagement
Activities planned but timing / logistics patchy
Research consistently informs engagement; findings feed back into further inquiry
Clear, balanced analysis; key factors explored
Effective synthesis; perspectives woven into coherent argument
Biases explored; impact on work examined
Logical structure; mostly easy to follow
